result.markdown:
--- title: "Data wrangling I" subtitle: "POL51" author: "Haley Daarstad" institute: "University of California, Davis" date: today date-format: long format: revealjs: chalkboard: true slide-number: true height: 900 width: 1600 code-line-numbers: true smooth-scrawl: true theme: [simple, html/custom.scss] incremental: true title-slide-attributes: data-background-image: img/dubois-spiral-2.png data-background-size: contain data-background-position: right data-background-color: "#dc354a" --- # Plan for today {.center background-color="#dc354a"} Wrangling and pipes . . . Subsetting data . . . The (tricky!) programming objects # The new starting point {background-color="#dc354a"} . . . Before, I *wrangled* data and you plotted the finished product . . . First step of all your code was `ggplot()` . . . Now, **you** will wrangle the data . . . First step is now *the data object* ## What is data-wrangling? . . . > ...the process of transforming and mapping data from one "raw" data form into another format with the intent of making it more appropriate and valuable for a variety of downstream purposes such as analytics... **Data analysts typically spend the majority of their time in the process of data wrangling compared to the actual analysis of the data.** -- Wikipedia . . . Most of your time working with data will be spent **wrangling** it into a usable form for analysis ## Pipes: connecting data to functions ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} directors_profit = movies |> # only look at horror movies filter(genre1 == "Horror" | genre2 == "Horror" | genre3 == "Horror") |> # calculate profit mutate(profit = gross - budget) ``` :::
You've seen these before... ## What are pipes? ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - **Pipes** link **data** to **functions** - They look like this `%>%`, or `|>` - Definitely use keyboard shortcuts - OSX: Cmd + Shift + M - Windows: Ctrl + Shift + M ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/pipes.png) ::: ::::: ## Why pipes? . . . With pipes: 😍 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} penguins %>% filter(species == "Adelie") %>% mutate(body_mass_kg = body_mass_g / 1000) %>% select(body_mass_kg) ``` ::: . . . Without pipes: 🤢 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} select(mutate(filter(penguins, species == "Adelie"), body_mass_kg = body_mass_g / 1000), body_mass_kg) ``` ::: . . .
Both produce the same output, but pipes make code more **legible** ## Making sense of pipes: "and then..." ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} directors_profit = movies %>% # only look at horror movies filter(genre1 == "Horror" | genre2 == "Horror" | genre3 == "Horror") %>% # calculate profit mutate(profit = gross - budget) ``` ::: . . .
You can read the pipe as if it said "*and then*"... . . . 1. Take the data object `movies`, AND THEN 2. `filter` so genre1, genre2, or genre3 equal HORROR, AND THEN 3. `mutate` so that... # Subsetting data and logical operators {background-color="#dc354a"} ## Our first wrangling function: `filter()` . . . `filter()` **subsets** data objects based on **rules** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/filter.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} baby_subset <- babynames %>% filter(name == "Angel") ``` ::: Subset `babynames` to only babies named `Angel` # Why filter? {.center background-color="#dc354a"} ## Why filter? {.center} . . . Lots of real-world **applications**: finding flights, addresses, IDs, etc. . . . Sometimes we want to focus on a specific **subset** of data: the South, Latin America, etc. . . . Useful to deal with common problems: outliers, missing data, strange responses ## The Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} Third largest welfare program in the US Only people who meet certain **criteria** receive it **Effects** of program and its **design** are hotly debated ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/eitc.png) ::: ::::: ::: notes What does this graph tell us about the criteria? ::: ## Identifying beneficiaries Imagine you are the IRS, and have data on all 360+ million Americans: . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Sex Race Age Income Marital Children
Male White 67 17643 Married 1
Female White 39 24689 Not married 1
Female White 76 121239 Not married 1
Female White 54 41001 Married 0
Male White 72 92432 Not married 1
Female White 77 64430 Not married 1
Female White 78 580 Not married 1
````` ::: ::: . . . How could use use these variables to **identify** what benefits they should receive? ## Identifying beneficiaries Say we wanted to identify people in the flat part of the [blue line]{.blue} ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}
Income Marital Children
17642.88 Married 1
24688.98 Not married 1
121239.34 Not married 1
41001.08 Married 0
92431.88 Not married 1
64430.12 Not married 1
580.02 Not married 1
````` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ![](img/eitc.png) ::: ::::: ## Using `filter()` To use `filter()`, we need to tell R which **observations** we want to include (or exclude) using *rules* . . .
::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gap_africa = gapminder |> filter(region == "Africa") ``` ::: Rule: return all observations from `gapminder` where the `region` variable **is equal to** "Africa" ## Making the rules: logical operators ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} - Rules filter data based on whether **variables** meet certain criteria - Rules rely on **logical operators**: - Equal to, not equal to, less than, more than, included in, etc. - Observations that meet the rule are returned; those that are not are **dropped** ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/ben.jpeg){fig-align='center' width=90%} ::: ::: ::: ::::: ## The logical operators ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} | Operator | meaning | |:--------:|:-----------------------------:| | == | equal to | | != | not equal to | | > | greater than | | < | less than | | >= | greater than or equal to | | <= | less than or equal to | | & | AND (both conditions true) | | | | OR (either condition is true) | | %in% | IN (in the set of) | ::: ::: ::: notes Why double equal sign? ::: ## Using `filter()` Say we have some data on 🍎 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} |name |color | pounds|sweet | |:------------|:-----|------:|:-----| |Fuji |red | 2|TRUE | |Gala |green | 4|TRUE | |Macintosh |green | 8|FALSE | |Granny Smith |red | 3|FALSE | ::: ::: ## Apples ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Fuji red 2 TRUE 2 Gala green 4 TRUE 3 Macintosh green 8 FALSE 4 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ::: ::: callout-note The output reports how many rows and columns our dataset has (4 rows x 4 columns) ::: ## Green apples ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(color == "green") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Gala green 4 TRUE 2 Macintosh green 8 FALSE ``` ::: ::: . . . Notice words are in quotations! . . . Notice that the number of rows has decreased: `2 x 4` ## Green and unsweet apples ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(color == "green") |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Macintosh green 8 FALSE ``` ::: ::: Notice TRUE/FALSE are all-caps! ## Apples that aren't green ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(color != "green") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Fuji red 2 TRUE 2 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ::: The ! symbol **negates**: *not* equal to ## At least 4 pounds but less than 6 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(pounds >= 4, pounds < 6) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Gala green 4 TRUE ``` ::: ::: Notice: **at least** implies *greater than or equal to* . . . I could also split this up over multiple `filter` calls ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(pounds >= 4) |> filter(pounds < 6) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Gala green 4 TRUE ``` ::: ::: . . . ## Combinations: The OR (\|) operator "Observations where either *this* is true **OR** *that* is true" . . . Apples that are red OR green ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(color == "red" | color == "green") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Fuji red 2 TRUE 2 Gala green 4 TRUE 3 Macintosh green 8 FALSE 4 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ::: ::: callout-note The \| should be above your Return/Enter key ::: ## Combinations: the AND operator (&) The `&` operator can be used to combine rules . . . Returns observations where *both* rules are true . . . "Apples that are red AND sweet or green AND sour": . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(color == "red" & sweet == TRUE | color == "green" & sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: ## %in% The `%in%` operator is super powerful . . . It returns observations that belong to a **set** . . . Say I wanted to look at just South American countries, normally: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} gapminder |> filter(country == "Argentina" | country == "Brazil" | country == "Chile") ``` ::: Brutally repetitive ## %in% Make a list of countries and return observations that match any of them ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} keep = c("Argentina", "Brazil", "Chile") gapminder |> filter(country %in% keep) ``` ::: ::: callout-note To make a "list" of items (a **vector**), use `c()` ::: ## Your turn: 👑 World leaders 👑 Open an r script and download two new packages: `remotes` and `juanr`. Next Using the `leader` dataset, identify: 1. A Vietnamese Emperor who, in his first year in office, was 11 years old. Famously depraved. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("hail2thief/juanr") library(juanr) ``` ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: ::: ::: callout-note You can use `?leader` to see the codebook. The acronym for Vietnam is "VNM" ::: ## 👑 World leaders 👑 ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} library(juanr) ?leader leader |> filter(country == "VNM" & yr_office <= 1 & age == 11) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 1 × 16 country gwcode leader gender year yr_office age edu mil_service combat 1 VNM 815 Thanh Th… M 1889 1 11 Seco… 0 0 # ℹ 6 more variables: rebel , yrs_exp , phys_health , # mental_health , will_force , will_force_sd ``` ::: ::: # Objects {background-color="#dc354a"} ## Objects and Pipes Step 1-2: the data, the pipe, the wrangling functions ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` :::
. . . Step 3: store the subsetted data as a new **object** for later use ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} green_apples = apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: ## Reminder on Objects In programming, **objects** can be used to store all sorts of stuff for later use . . . data, functions, values . . . We create objects using `=` or `<-` . . . Like this: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} new_object = stuff |> filter(year == 1999) ``` ::: . . . Or like this: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} new_object <- stuff |> filter(year == 1999) ``` ::: ## Naming objects > *There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.* -- Phil Karlton . . . Recommend: keep it short, easy to type, informative, and use `_` to separate words . . . ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Good gap_africa = gapminder |> filter(continent == "Africa") # Bad Countries_In_Africa_That_I_Want_To_Look_Up = gapminder |> filter(continent == "Africa") ``` ::: . . . I use the excellent [Tidyverse syntax guide](https://style.tidyverse.org/syntax.html) in my work ## Reminder: no object, no save Without objects, your work washes away, like tears in the rain . . . ::::::: columns :::: {.column width="50%"} Here, we store our data wrangling ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} green_apples = apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) green_apples ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Macintosh green 8 FALSE 2 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: :::: {.column width="50%"} Here we didn't store ::: fragment ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 2 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Macintosh green 8 FALSE 2 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} apples ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 4 × 4 name color pounds sweet 1 Fuji red 2 TRUE 2 Gala green 4 TRUE 3 Macintosh green 8 FALSE 4 Granny Smith red 3 FALSE ``` ::: ::: ::: :::: ::::::: . . . Notice the original `apples` remains unchanged! ## The formula *Wrangle* the data until you're satisfied with the output: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: . . . Store the output as a new object: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} sour_apples = apples |> filter(sweet == FALSE) ``` ::: . . . Use the new object (e.g., plotting): ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} ggplot(sour_apples, aes(x = name, y = pounds)) + geom_col() ``` ::: ## Challenge: 🗳️ The (unusual) American voter 🗳️ There's a Twitter bot that randomly tweet profiles of real voters from the Cooperative Election Study: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](img/american-voter-bot.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Challenge: 🗳️ The (unusual) American voter 🗳️ ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} Table: A small sample of Americans |state |sex | age|educ |race |pid7 |ideo5 |religion |votechoice |hispanic |know_governor |conceal |prochoice |cleanair |wall |mandmin |aca |minwage |newsint | |:------------|:------|---:|:--------------------|:-----|:-----------------|:-----------------|:---------------------|:----------------------------|:--------|:-------------|:-------|:---------|:--------|:-------|:-------|:-------|:-------|:-----------------| |Washington |Male | 66|4-year |White |Strong Republican |Very conservative |Protestant |Donald J. Trump (Republican) |No |Democrat |Support |Oppose |Oppose |Support |Oppose |Support |Oppose |Most of the time | |Pennsylvania |Male | 68|High school graduate |Black |Independent |Liberal |Nothing in particular |Joe Biden (Democrat) |No |Democrat |Support |Oppose |Support |Support |Oppose |Support |Oppose |Only now and then | |Arizona |Female | 70|2-year |White |Strong Republican |Very conservative |Protestant |Donald J. Trump (Republican) |No |Republican |Oppose |Oppose |Support |Support |Oppose |Support |Oppose |Some of the time | |Arizona |Male | 57|High school graduate |White |Lean Republican |Very conservative |Protestant |Donald J. Trump (Republican) |No |Republican |Support |Oppose |Oppose |Support |Support |Support |Favor |Most of the time | |Connecticut |Male | 21|Some college |White |Strong Republican |Conservative |Nothing in particular |NA |No |Democrat |Support |Support |Oppose |Support |Support |Support |Oppose |Most of the time | ::: ::: ## 🗳️ The (unusual) American voter 🗳️ In your teams, using `bot`: 1. Identify the most **unusual** subgroup of voters you can think of 2. Constraint: need *at least* five voters in your subgroup 3. Store your unusual subgroup as an object 4. One member tells the class what was your subgroup of voters. ::: callout-note Remember you can use `?bot` to look at the codebook ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html} ``` ::: :::

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
